content:
As **feature engineering** matured from ad hoc spreadsheet columns to reproducible **ML pipelines**, scaling stopped being a one-line `StandardScaler` call and became a **branch decision**: which engineered numerics feed distance- or penalty-based models, and which paths serve scale-invariant trees.

Early **k-NN** and **clustering** workflows exposed that engineered ratios could still dwarf companion columns in Euclidean distance unless a scaler sat after construction. **Penalized regression** (Ridge, Lasso) made the same point for engineered logs and counts: penalties assume comparable variance across inputs.

**scikit-learn** `Pipeline` and `ColumnTransformer` (2010s) let teams encode that decision structurally—numeric branch with optional scaler, categorical branch with encoder—rather than scaling entire design matrices by hand. The **preprocessing** topic later became the canonical home for StandardScaler and MinMaxScaler **definitions**; feature-engineering topics focus on **where** those transforms belong after ratios, interactions, and domain aggregates are built.

Modern FE practice treats scaling as **algorithm-sensitive pipeline wiring** linked to, not duplicated from, canonical scaler documentation.

timeline.label: Hand-built design matrices
timeline.description: Analysts scaled selected columns in spreadsheets before feeding linear models—easy to misapply when new engineered ratios were added.

timeline.label: sklearn ColumnTransformer
timeline.description: Branch-level numeric vs categorical pipelines made scale-on-numerics-only the default pattern for mixed engineered tables.

timeline.label: Canonical split of concerns
timeline.description: Scaler formulas centralized in preprocessing topics; feature engineering owns placement after engineered features and algorithm-sensitive include rules.
